sections.heading: In most regions, many women wanted no more children
sections.body: In 2025, 49.4 percent of women nationwide wanted no more children, and most regions were close to or above that level. The highest group was Bicol Region at 57.0 percent, MIMAROPA at 56.9 percent, and Negros Island Region at 56.7 percent. Central Visayas followed at 54.7 percent. This shows that the main regional difference was not just one unusually high region. It was a wider group of regions where stopping childbearing was already the most common preference.

sections.heading: BARMM looked different from the national pattern
sections.body: BARMM had the lowest share of women who wanted no more children in 2025, at 27.3 percent. That was far below the national level of 49.4 percent. BARMM also had the highest undecided share, at 27.8 percent, so being undecided was about as common as wanting no more children. This was different from regions where wanting no more children was clearly the main response, and it suggests a less settled pattern of fertility preferences.

sections.heading: In Zamboanga Peninsula, fewer women wanted to stop, and more wanted to wait
sections.body: Zamboanga Peninsula¹ was near the low end for wanting no more children, at 39.9 percent in 2025. Unlike BARMM, its biggest difference was not a high undecided share. It was wanting another child later, where Zamboanga Peninsula ranked highest in the country at 23.5 percent. Taken together, this makes the region look less like a simple case of low stopping preference and more like a place where waiting to have another child remained a major part of the preference mix.

sections.heading: The biggest regional changes moved in opposite directions
sections.body: Cagayan Valley had the largest increase in the share of women wanting no more children, rising from 39.8 percent in 2022 to 49.4 percent in 2025. BARMM also rose sharply, from 18.6 to 27.3 percent, although it still had the lowest level among regions in 2025. Moving the other way, Zamboanga Peninsula fell from 54.9 to 39.9 percent. That drop was much larger than the declines in Bicol Region, Western Visayas, or Eastern Visayas.

sections.heading: National preferences barely changed, but more women were unsure
sections.body: Nationally, the share of women wanting no more children was almost unchanged, moving from 48.8 percent in 2022 to 49.4 percent in 2025. The shares wanting another child soon and wanting another child later both edged down, from 13.9 to 13.4 percent and from 17.4 to 16.2 percent. The clearest change was in the undecided category, which increased from 8.1 to 10.0 percent, even as sterilization and declared infecund shares declined slightly.

sections.heading: Preferences changed most clearly as family size grew
sections.body: The number-of-living-children table shows a sharp shift from wanting another child to wanting no more children, with current pregnancies included in the count. Among women with no living children, 54.9 percent wanted another child soon and only 6.2 percent wanted no more children. By two children, wanting no more children had reached 57.5 percent. Among women with six or more children, it rose further to 72.8 percent.

takeaways: In 2025, wanting no more children was the most common preference nationally, but regional levels ranged from 27.3 percent in BARMM to 57.0 percent in Bicol Region.
BARMM stood out because being undecided was almost as common as wanting no more children.
Preferences changed sharply by family size: once women had two living children, wanting no more children became the majority response.
footnotes: ¹ Includes the province of Sulu in 2025.
